title: Mesopotamian extispicy — reading the liver
blurb: Babylonian bārû-priests read the future in the entrails of sacrificed animals, mapping the liver against the sky. This is the direct ancestor of Etruscan and Roman haruspicy.
mtg: The gut-gazing diviner is the oldest form of the seer MTG paints in blue and white.
source: Jean Bottéro, Mesopotamia: Writing, Reasoning, and the Gods

title: Egyptian heka — magic as a cosmic force
blurb: In Egyptian thought heka was not illicit but a fundamental power of creation, wielded by gods and priests alike. Magic and religion were one system.
mtg: The idea that magic is a neutral, color-blind energy underlies MTG's whole mana system.
source: Geraldine Pinch, Magic in Ancient Egypt

title: Egyptian amulets and the Book of the Dead
blurb: Funerary papyri specify amulets — the scarab, the ankh, the wedjat eye — to be placed on the body, each a consecrated object of protection for the journey through death.
mtg: The oldest wearable technology; the ancestor of every protective artifact.
source: Geraldine Pinch, Magic in Ancient Egypt

title: Zoroaster and the Persian magi
blurb: The maguš, the Zoroastrian priestly caste of Persia, tended fire and read the stars. Their name would become the Greek magos and the root of magic, magician, and mage.
mtg: Every 'mage' subtype on a card descends, by name, from this Persian priesthood.
source: Etymonline, s.v. magi; RenaissanceMagicDB context

title: Homer's nekuia — Odysseus questions the dead
blurb: In Odyssey XI, Odysseus digs a pit, fills it with blood, and summons the shades to speak. This is necromancy in its original sense: divination BY the dead, not the raising of them.
mtg: The reanimator is a modern mutation; the original necromancer merely listened.
source: Homer, Odyssey XI; Daniel Ogden, Greek and Roman Necromancy

title: Etruscan haruspicy passes to Rome
blurb: The Etruscans bequeathed to Rome the disciplina of the haruspex — reading livers against a bronze template of the heavens. The Piacenza Liver survives as its map.
source: Luck, Arcana Mundi

title: The Sibylline tradition
blurb: The Sibyls — freelance prophetesses at Cumae, Delphi, Erythrae — spoke ecstatic oracles. Rome kept Sibylline Books consulted in crisis for a thousand years.
mtg: The prophetess who out-ranked the institutional oracle.
source: H. W. Parke, Sibyls and Sibylline Prophecy

title: Greek curse tablets (katadesmoi / defixiones)
blurb: Thin lead sheets inscribed with binding curses — against rivals, lovers, chariot teams — were folded, pierced, and buried. Thousands survive: the archaeology of the everyday curse.
mtg: The oldest evidence of magic as a hostile, targeted act — an Aura you'd never want.
source: John Gager, Curse Tablets and Binding Spells from the Ancient World

title: The goēs — the sorcerer who howls
blurb: Archaic Greek knew the goēs, a wailing ritual specialist who conducted souls and worked charms with cries. His art, goēteia, became the low, coercive magic opposed to philosophy.
source: RenaissanceMagicDB, s.v. goetia; Sarah Iles Johnston, Restless Dead

title: Herodotus describes the magi
blurb: Herodotus' Histories give Greece its picture of the Persian magi as dream-interpreters and sacrificers — the first ethnography of a magical priesthood.
source: Herodotus, Histories I

title: The Eleusinian Mysteries at their height
blurb: Initiates (mystai) at Eleusis swore silence and 'closed their eyes' to the outer world — the root sense of mystic, from myein, 'to shut.' The mysteries promised a blessed afterlife.
mtg: The initiate-only knowledge that gives us 'mystic' and 'mystery.'
source: Walter Burkert, Ancient Mystery Cults

title: Aristotle's fifth element (aether)
blurb: Beyond earth, water, air and fire, Aristotle posited a fifth, incorruptible element composing the heavens. Latinised as the quinta essentia, it became the alchemists' goal.
source: Aristotle, De Caelo; Lyndy Abraham, A Dictionary of Alchemical Imagery

title: Theophrastus and the 'characters'
blurb: Theophrastus' Characters sketched moral types; the Greek charaktēr, 'an engraved mark,' would later name the sigil-signs inscribed on talismans to seize a power.
source: RenaissanceMagicDB, s.v. characteres

title: Berossus brings Babylonian astrology to Greece
blurb: The Babylonian priest Berossus founded a school on Kos, carrying celestial divination into the Greek world, where it fused with philosophy into learned astrology.
source: Tamsyn Barton, Ancient Astrology

title: Bolus of Mendes and sympathetic magic
blurb: Bolus' Physika systematised the doctrine of hidden sympathies and antipathies among things — the theory that lets a talisman work, and proto-alchemy begin.
source: Luck, Arcana Mundi

title: The Greek Magical Papyri (PGM) tradition
blurb: A cache of Greco-Egyptian papyri preserves working spells: recipes for love, dreams, divination, and the compelling of gods. The single richest source for ancient magical practice.
mtg: The actual spellbooks of antiquity — where 'spell' means a formula that does something.
source: Hans Dieter Betz, The Greek Magical Papyri in Translation

title: Cicero's De Divinatione
blurb: Cicero's dialogue weighs — and largely doubts — the arts of divination even as it catalogues them: augury, haruspicy, dreams, oracles. The first great skeptical treatise on foretelling.
source: Cicero, De Divinatione

title: Roman state augury
blurb: No public act began without the augurs taking the auspices — reading the will of the gods in the flight and feeding of birds. The ritual survives in the word 'inauguration.'
mtg: Divination as statecraft; the bird-omen behind every 'auspicious' beginning.
source: RenaissanceMagicDB context; Luck, Arcana Mundi

title: Simon Magus and the Christian polemic against magic
blurb: The Acts of the Apostles casts Simon 'the magus' as a false wonder-worker, fixing the magus as a Christian byword for illicit power — 'simony' is named for him.
source: Acts 8; Kieckhefer, Magic in the Middle Ages

title: Pliny's Natural History on amulets and magic
blurb: Pliny's encyclopaedia records — while sneering at — hundreds of magical remedies, amulets, and the 'vanity of the magi,' preserving a vast trove of popular practice.
source: Pliny, Natural History XXVIII–XXX

title: The Corpus Hermeticum
blurb: Greek philosophical dialogues attributed to Hermes Trismegistus fuse Platonism, Egyptian religion and cosmic piety. Their rediscovery would ignite the Renaissance revival of magic.
source: Brian Copenhaver, Hermetica; RenaissanceMagicDB, s.v. hermetica

title: Ptolemy's Tetrabiblos
blurb: Ptolemy gave astrology its authoritative technical form — houses, aspects, dignities — the framework still used to elect the hour for a talisman.
source: Ptolemy, Tetrabiblos; RenaissanceMagicDB, s.vv. aspectus, dignitas

title: Artemidorus' Oneirocritica
blurb: Artemidorus compiled the ancient world's great dream-book, a systematic key to oneiromancy that Freud himself would cite.
source: Artemidorus, Oneirocritica

title: Zosimos of Panopolis — the first named alchemist
blurb: Zosimos' visionary treatises, full of dreams of transformation and apparatus, are the earliest substantial alchemical writings under a known author — Greco-Egyptian khēmeia.
mtg: The origin point of the alchemy the game turns into a mechanic.
source: Lyndy Abraham, A Dictionary of Alchemical Imagery; Principe, The Secrets of Alchemy

title: Plotinus and the Enneads
blurb: Plotinus founded Neoplatonism, mapping reality as emanation from the One through Nous and Soul — the metaphysics that would underwrite theurgy and Renaissance magic alike.
source: Plotinus, Enneads; RenaissanceMagicDB, s.vv. nous, emanatio

title: Iamblichus' De Mysteriis defends theurgy
blurb: Against Porphyry's doubts, Iamblichus argued that ritual 'god-working' (theourgia) could lift the soul to the divine — the philosophical charter of high magic.
mtg: The high-magic/low-magic split the game mostly erased.
source: RenaissanceMagicDB, s.v. theurgia; Gregory Shaw, Theurgy and the Soul

title: Proclus systematises theurgy
blurb: The last great pagan Neoplatonist, Proclus, wove theurgic ritual into a total metaphysical system, transmitting it to Byzantium and, eventually, to Ficino.
source: RenaissanceMagicDB, s.v. theurgia

title: Augustine condemns divination and omens
blurb: In De Doctrina Christiana, Augustine folds augury, astrology and omen-reading into idolatry and demonic pact — the frame that would criminalise magic for a millennium.
source: Augustine, De Doctrina Christiana; Kieckhefer, Magic in the Middle Ages

title: The Testament of Solomon
blurb: This Greek text has Solomon binding and interrogating demons with a magic ring — the seed of the whole Solomonic tradition that flowers in the medieval grimoires.
mtg: The template for binding named demons — the Ars Goetia's ancestor.
source: Joseph Peterson (ed.), The Testament of Solomon; RenaissanceMagicDB context
